text:
# Cybercrime Types Glossary

Reference definitions for common cybercrime/fraud terms a victim or reporting
officer might encounter. Use this to explain unfamiliar terms, not as scam-
specific advice (see the other knowledge base files for that).

## Business E-Mail Compromise (BEC)

A scam targeting businesses that regularly do international wire transfers.
Fraudsters compromise or spoof a legitimate business e-mail account (via
social engineering or intrusion) to redirect a real payment to an account they
control. Email Account Compromise (EAC) is the same tactic aimed at
individuals making online transactions with foreign entities.

## Ransomware

Malware that encrypts a victim's files or locks their device, demanding
payment (often in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Unlike malware that
quietly steals data, ransomware is overtly disruptive and targets everything
from individual devices to large organizations.

## Email spoofing

Forging an e-mail header so a message appears to come from someone other than
the real sender. Used heavily in phishing because people are more likely to
open and trust mail that looks like it's from someone they know.

## Phishing / Vishing / Smishing

- **Phishing** — fraudulent e-mail impersonating a legitimate source (bank,
  recruiter, company) to extract sensitive information.
- **Vishing** — the same approach by phone call.
- **Smishing** — the same approach by SMS, usually with a link or callback
  number.

## Spear phishing

A targeted form of phishing aimed at specific individuals (often in a
company), using personal details — real name, rank, designation — to make the
message convincing. Spear-phishing e-mails often carry malicious attachments
(XLS/PDF/DOC) that connect to a remote command-and-control server once
opened, and frequently drop a harmless-looking decoy document to mask the
attack.

## Illegal online transactions

Covers fraudulent/unauthorized transactions, claims of lost or stolen
merchandise, and false requests for a refund or return — generally any scheme
where a fraudster poses as a legitimate party to extract money or sensitive
card/account details online.

## Job fraud

Deceiving job seekers with false promises of high salary or easy income to
extract money (fees, deposits) or personal information.

## Cyber defamation

Publishing or transmitting, in electronic form, any statement or image
intended to harm a person's or organization's reputation — the digital
equivalent of traditional defamation.

## Ponzi scheme

An investment scam that pays early "returns" using new investors' money
rather than real profit, collapsing once new investment dries up. Promises of
high returns with little risk are the hallmark warning sign.

## Cyber stalking / cyber bullying

Cyber stalking is persistent harassment of a victim using electronic
communication (e-mail, messaging, social posts). Cyber bullying is repeated,
deliberate harmful or humiliating content about a specific person, spread via
SMS, social media, or other digital channels.

## Cyber pornography

Using cyberspace to create, view, distribute, or publish pornography or
obscene material; a distinct legal offense from other fraud types but often
encountered alongside blackmail/extortion scams (e.g. sextortion).

## Hacking, virus, worm, trojan

- **Hacking** — exploiting weaknesses to gain unauthorized access to a
  computer system or network.
- **Virus** — code that attaches to another program/file and activates when
  that file runs, damaging or degrading the system.
- **Worm** — self-replicating malicious software that spreads across
  connected devices without needing a host file.
- **Trojan** — malware disguised as legitimate software (e.g. a fake office
  app or game) that carries a hidden malicious purpose.

## Website defacement / web jacking

Website defacement is altering a site's visible content (often with hostile
or obscene material) to embarrass its owner or make a political statement.
Web jacking is a phishing variant where attackers clone a real website and
send victims a malicious link to the fake version to harvest credentials or
card details.

## Salami attack

A scheme that makes many small, individually unnoticeable alterations to a
system or account (e.g. fractions of a rupee skimmed from many transactions)
that add up to significant financial gain for the attacker.

## DoS / DDoS attacks

A Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack overwhelms a website or server with traffic
so legitimate users can't access it; a Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)
attack does the same using many systems at once, making it harder to block.

## Card fraud categories

- **Lost/stolen card fraud** — a lost or stolen card is used by whoever finds
  or takes it.
- **Counterfeit/skimming fraud** — card data is copied (skimmed) onto a
  duplicate card without the owner's knowledge.
- **Card-not-received fraud** — a newly issued card is intercepted in transit
  (e.g. by a courier accomplice or using fake ID) before reaching the real
  owner.
- **Set-up company fraud** — fraudsters rent an address purely to apply for
  cards across multiple banks, collect them, and disappear.

# Digital Arrest Scam — Core Pattern

A "digital arrest" scam is a phone or video call in which a fraudster
impersonates a law-enforcement or regulatory authority (CBI, ED, Customs,
TRAI, local police, or a courier company acting on their behalf) and
convinces the victim that they are under investigation or "digital arrest"
and must stay on the call, follow instructions, and pay money to avoid
arrest. Real Indian law-enforcement agencies never conduct arrests, demand
payments, or hold "virtual hearings" over WhatsApp, Skype, or a phone call.
There is no such legal concept as a "digital arrest" in Indian law.

## How the scam typically unfolds

1. **Intro / hook** — The victim gets a call claiming a parcel/SIM/bank
   account in their name is linked to an illegal shipment (drugs, fake
   passports) or financial crime. The caller often spoofs a number to look
   like it's from a local police station or courier company.
2. **Authority handoff** — The call escalates to someone impersonating a CBI,
   ED, or police officer, sometimes in a fake uniform on video, with a
   forged ID card or "warrant" shown on screen.
3. **Threat and urgency** — The victim is told they face immediate arrest,
   asset freeze, or a non-bailable case unless they cooperate right now. The
   pressure is deliberately constant so the victim has no time to think or
   verify independently.
4. **Isolation instructions** — The victim is told not to disconnect the
   call, not to tell family members, and sometimes to stay on camera
   continuously ("digital arrest") while moving to a private room.
5. **Extraction** — The victim is asked to transfer money to "verify
   accounts" or pay a "fine" to avoid arrest, sometimes via multiple small
   transfers to avoid bank fraud triggers, or asked to install remote-access
   apps (AnyDesk, TeamViewer) so the scammer can directly operate the
   victim's banking apps.

## Red flags to listen for

- Caller claims to be CBI, ED, Customs, TRAI, or police and threatens arrest
  over a phone/video call.
- Instructions to not hang up, not tell anyone, or stay on camera
  continuously.
- Any request to install AnyDesk, TeamViewer, or similar remote-access apps.
- Any request for OTPs, Aadhaar number, bank PINs, or to transfer money to
  "verify" an account or avoid a "non-bailable warrant."
- Pressure to act within minutes, with no opportunity to call the agency
  back on an officially listed number.

## What to tell a worried caller

- No real police, CBI, ED, or Customs officer will ever arrest someone over
  a phone or video call, or ask for money/OTP to "avoid arrest."
- They should hang up immediately, not share any OTP, PIN, or install any
  remote-access app, and not transfer any money.
- If money has already been transferred, they should call the **1930**
  cybercrime helpline immediately (the faster a complaint is filed, the
  higher the chance of freezing the transferred funds) and file a report at
  **cybercrime.gov.in**.
- They can independently verify any claimed case by visiting the local
  police station in person or contacting the agency through its official
  website — never through a number or link given by the caller.

# Identity Theft

Identity theft is the act of wrongfully obtaining someone's personal information
(name, phone number, address, bank account number, Aadhaar number, credit/debit
card number) without their permission. Stolen identity information can be used to
access bank accounts, apply for loans or credit cards, file a fraudulent tax
refund, obtain a driver's license or passport in the victim's name, get medical
treatment on the victim's health insurance, or impersonate the victim on social
media and even to the police during an arrest.

## Hacking or gaining access to social media accounts

An attacker who hacks or otherwise gains access to a victim's social media
account can misuse personal information and photographs, post offensive or
morphed content on the victim's profile, or use it to defame the victim — for
example, leaking confidential documents under the victim's name or posting
manipulated obscene images. A common entry point is leaving an account logged
in on a shared computer, such as at a cybercafe.

**Safety tips:** always log out of accounts on shared computers rather than just
closing the browser window; use two-factor authentication (OTP) when using
someone else's computer; never save your username/password in a shared
browser; register your mobile number with social platforms to get alerts on
unauthorized access; permanently delete any documents you downloaded at a
cybercafe before leaving.

## Misuse of photocopies of identity proofs

Sharing photocopies of identity documents (PAN card, Aadhaar, IT returns) with
an untrusted agency — for example, a non-reputed loan agent offering unusually
low interest rates — can let a fraudster apply for multiple loans in the
victim's name across several banks, by altering the photograph, signature,
address, or phone number on the copy while keeping the real document number.

**Safety tips:** never give copies of identity proofs to unknown people or
organizations; be cautious about which proofs you submit and where; strike
through a photocopy and write the specific purpose of its use directly on it so
it can't be reused for another purpose; don't share sensitive personal details
(date of birth, address, family details) on public platforms.

## Credit/debit card skimming

A skimmer is a small device that secretly copies the data on a card's magnetic
stripe — name, card number, expiry date — when the card is swiped, commonly at
restaurants, petrol pumps, or ATMs. The captured data is used to clone the card
and withdraw money or make online transactions. PIN theft often accompanies
skimming: someone nearby (e.g. a waiter handling the card) watches and notes
the PIN as it's entered.

**Safety tips:** keep your card in sight during any swipe transaction — don't
let staff carry it out of view; be alert for card readers that protrude
oddly from a payment machine, a sign of a skimmer; never share your PIN with
anyone, however trusted; cover the keypad with your other hand while entering
a PIN; check ATMs for signs of tampering (new scratches, loose parts, hidden
cameras) before use.

# Psychological Tricks (Phishing, Vishing, Smishing)

Psychological tricks exploit urgency, fear, or excitement to get a victim to
hand over money or sensitive information, typically via fake e-mails, calls, or
SMS.

- **Phishing** — fraudulent e-mail appearing to be from a legitimate source
  (a bank, recruiter, credit card company) aiming to extract sensitive
  information.
- **Vishing** — the same tactic over a phone call instead of e-mail.
- **Smishing** — the same tactic via SMS, usually pushing the recipient to a
  fraudulent website or phone number.

## Lottery fraud

The victim is told (by e-mail/call/SMS) that they've won a lottery, and is
asked to pay a "token amount" or share personal/bank details to release the
winnings. No money is ever released; the victim only loses the amount sent.

**Safety tips:** you cannot win a lottery you never entered; never respond to
unsolicited messages asking for personal or financial details, even if they
promise a credit to your account; verify any government-linked claim against
an official ".gov.in"/".nic.in" domain, not the link in the message; enable
spam filters on your e-mail.

## Credit/debit card fraud (vishing)

A caller claims to be from the victim's bank, says the card has been "blocked
due to suspicious activity," and asks for the card number and PIN to
"re-activate" it. Banks already have these details and never need to ask for
them — the caller may sound convincing by citing a few real details (name,
city, that you hold an account there) to build false trust.

**Safety tips:** a real bank will never call to say your card is blocked and
ask you to "verify" it by giving the card number, PIN, CVV, or OTP; if you're
worried about your card, hang up and call the number printed on the back of
the card yourself.

## Job-related fraud

A fake e-mail offers an attractive job with a high salary, asks for personal
details, and sometimes asks the victim to travel to a specific location/hotel
for an "interview" — which can escalate into being drugged, robbed, or worse
in some reported cases.

**Safety tips:** apply for jobs only through authentic portals or known
recruiters; check that the sender's e-mail domain matches the company's real
domain; treat spelling/grammar errors in a recruiting e-mail as a red flag;
never pay money or travel to an unfamiliar location as a condition of a job
offer.

# Social Media Frauds

## Sympathy and romance fraud

A fraudster befriends the victim on social media, builds trust or affection
over time through frequent chats and calls, then asks for money — citing a
medical emergency or similar urgent need — or, in romance-fraud cases,
manipulates the victim into an intimate relationship and later blackmails them
with photos/videos.

**Safety tips:** be cautious accepting friend requests from strangers; never
send money or share intimate photos with someone you've only met online;
never get into financial dealings with someone you know only through social
media; if you plan to meet an online friend in person, tell family/friends and
meet in a public place.

## Cyber stalking and cyber bullying

Cyber stalking is harassment carried out through electronic means (e-mail, IM,
social posts), often by someone tracking the victim's real-world movements
through what they post (e.g. travel check-ins). Cyber bullying is repeated,
deliberate harmful content (posts, memes, troll pages) aimed at embarrassing
or humiliating a victim, common among teenagers.

**Safety tips:** restrict your social profile's visibility and avoid publicly
posting real-time location/travel plans; watch for behavioral changes in
children (withdrawal, fear of devices) as a sign of bullying or stalking; don't
delete abusive messages — they help in any later investigation; report
incidents to parents, school authorities, or police rather than staying
silent.

# Mobile Application and Online Banking Frauds

Malicious or infected mobile apps (often Trojan-laced clones of popular apps)
can access messages, OTPs, camera, and contacts, show intrusive ads, or sign
the user up for paid subscriptions without consent.

Weak or reused passwords are a common way online banking and other accounts
get hacked — e.g. a password is overheard or guessed (like a birth date), and
because the same password is reused across accounts, one breach compromises
several accounts at once.

**Safety tips:** install apps only from official stores and check permission
requests are sensible for the app's purpose; keep apps updated; use a unique,
strong password (8+ characters, mixed case/numbers/symbols) for every account;
never share your password with anyone, even someone you trust, since you
can't control who they repeat it to; enable 2-step verification (OTP) wherever
available; set a maximum transaction limit on bank/payment accounts so any
single compromise is capped.

# Virus Attacks on Personal Computers

Viruses commonly spread through infected external storage (USB/pen drives),
downloads from untrusted websites (disguised as music/video files), or
installation of pirated/unofficial software and games.

**Safety tips:** keep an updated antivirus/firewall running; scan external
storage devices before opening files from them; avoid pirated software; check
for "https" in the address bar before any online transaction; only download
software from official, trusted sources.

# Reporting, Helpline Facts & Universal Safety Rules

## Official reporting channels (verify against cybercrime.gov.in before relying on these)
- **1930** — national cybercrime helpline. Toll-free, available 24x7, works
  PAN-India with no STD code. Operated by the Indian Cyber Crime Coordination
  Centre (I4C) under the Ministry of Home Affairs. It replaced the older 155260
  number in 2021. Call it especially for financial fraud / money lost.
- **https://cybercrime.gov.in** — National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (NCRP).
  File a written complaint here; you receive an acknowledgement number and can
  attach evidence (screenshots, transaction IDs, a short written account).
- For losses above Rs 50,000 or identity theft, also approach your local cyber
  cell / police station with the portal acknowledgement.

## Why speed matters
Report as fast as possible. Freezing the money trail works far better within the
first 2-4 hours of the fraud. The sooner you report, the better the chance of
freezing the receiving account and recovering funds.

## Beware the "callback" follow-up scam
After reporting, some victims get a second call from a spoofed number claiming to
be 1930 or the bank's fraud cell: "Your case is registered — share the OTP to
process your refund" or "pay a small verification fee." This is a SECOND scam.
1930 will never call you from a 10-digit mobile number, never ask for an OTP, and
never ask for a fee to process a refund. If this happens, hang up and call 1930
yourself.

## Universal red flags across ALL scams
- Any request for an OTP, UPI PIN, full card number, CVV, or net-banking password.
  No legitimate bank, agency, or company ever needs these from you.
- Any demand to install a screen-sharing / remote-access app (AnyDesk, TeamViewer)
  on someone's instruction.
- Pressure and urgency: "act now," "within hours," "or you'll be arrested/blocked."
- A demand to pay money upfront to receive money (refund, prize, job, investment,
  parcel release, or to "prove innocence").
- Secrecy: being told not to tell family or to stay alone on a call.
- Being contacted from a number/link the caller provides and told to use only that.

## What to do if you have ALREADY been scammed
1. Call **1930** immediately — speed is critical.
2. Call your **bank** to block the card / account / report the unauthorized
   transaction and request a freeze on the beneficiary.
3. File a complaint at **https://cybercrime.gov.in**; save the acknowledgement PDF.
4. If you installed a remote-access app, uninstall it, disconnect the internet,
   and consider resetting the device; change banking passwords from a safe device.
5. Keep all evidence: SMS/WhatsApp screenshots, numbers, transaction IDs, the app/
   website used.

## Tone for assisting a worried person
Stay calm, clear, and non-alarmist. Reassure them that hanging up and reporting is
the right move, that no real officer arrests people over video calls, and that
acting quickly improves recovery. Never ask them to share sensitive details (OTP,
full card number, passwords) with you either.

# Digital Arrest Scam

## What it is
A "digital arrest" scam is a fraud where criminals impersonate law-enforcement or
government officers (CBI, ED, Customs, Narcotics Control Bureau, TRAI, police,
Mumbai/Delhi Cyber Cell) over a phone or video call. They falsely accuse the
victim of a serious crime, then keep them on a continuous video call for hours or
days under "digital arrest" — a fake legal status that does not exist in Indian
law — while extracting money. There is NO such thing as a "digital arrest" in
Indian law. No real agency arrests, interrogates, or holds anyone over a video
call.

## How the scam unfolds (typical stages)
1. **Intro:** Caller claims to be an officer of CBI/ED/Customs/police. May cite a
   fake case number, your real name, or partial Aadhaar to sound credible. Often
   begins as an automated call about a "parcel" or "SIM/number misuse."
2. **Threat:** Claims your Aadhaar, bank account, or a parcel is linked to money
   laundering, drugs, or a serious crime. Says a warrant is issued and you face
   immediate arrest.
3. **Isolation:** Orders you to stay on the call, keep your video on, go to a room
   alone, and tell NO ONE — not even family. This isolation is the core of the
   scam; it prevents you from getting a reality check.
4. **Extraction:** Demands you transfer money to a "verification" / "safe" / "RBI"
   account to "prove your innocence," promising a refund. Or demands OTP, Aadhaar,
   bank details, or that you install a screen-sharing app (AnyDesk, TeamViewer).

## Red flags (any ONE means it is almost certainly a scam)
- The words "digital arrest," "you cannot disconnect," "do not tell anyone."
- A demand to keep video on continuously or stay on the call for hours.
- A demand to transfer money to "verify," to an "RBI account," or a "safe account."
- A request to install AnyDesk / TeamViewer / any screen-share app.
- A request for OTP, full Aadhaar number, bank or card details.
- Threats of immediate arrest unless you act right now.
- Pressure to keep it secret from family.
- An "officer" who calls on a normal 10-digit mobile or international number.

## What real agencies actually do
- Real CBI, ED, Customs, police, or any agency NEVER arrest, interrogate, or
  detain anyone over a phone or video call.
- They NEVER ask you to transfer money to prove innocence. No legitimate
  investigation works this way.
- They NEVER ask you to keep a video call running or to hide the matter from
  family.
- Real summons/notices are delivered in writing/in person, and you have the right
  to consult a lawyer and verify at the actual office.
- A genuine investigation lets you contact the agency through official, publicly
  listed numbers — never a number the caller gives you.

## Correct advice
- Hang up. It is not rude to disconnect a scammer impersonating an officer.
- Do not transfer any money, share any OTP/Aadhaar/bank detail, or install any app.
- Do not stay on the call out of fear. No real officer can "arrest" you by phone.
- Tell a family member or friend immediately — secrecy is exactly what the scam
  needs.
- Verify independently: contact the real agency or your local police using their
  official public number, not the caller's number.
- Report it (see reporting steps below). Report fast — freezing fraudulent funds
  works far better within the first 2-4 hours.

## Reporting
- Call the national cybercrime helpline **1930** (toll-free, 24x7, works PAN-India).
- File a written complaint at **https://cybercrime.gov.in** (National Cyber Crime
  Reporting Portal). Use the mobile number involved; attach screenshots and a
  short written account. You receive an acknowledgement number.
- For losses above Rs 50,000 or identity theft, also visit your local cyber cell
  with the portal acknowledgement.

## Note on impersonation of the helpline itself
1930 will NEVER call you back from a 10-digit mobile number asking for an OTP or a
"verification fee" to "process your refund." If a 10-digit number claims to be
1930 or your bank's fraud cell and asks for OTP or payment, it is a follow-up
scam — hang up and call 1930 yourself.

# Courier / Customs Parcel Scam

## What it is
The victim gets a call (often starting as an automated/IVR message) claiming a
parcel in their name has been seized by Customs, FedEx, DHL, BlueDart, or the
police because it contains illegal items — drugs, fake passports, weapons, or
banned goods. The caller then either extorts a "fine/clearance fee" or escalates
into a full digital arrest scam (impersonating Customs or Narcotics officers).

## How the scam unfolds
1. Automated call: "A parcel sent in your name has been seized," press a key to
   "speak to an officer."
2. A fake Customs/FedEx/police officer says the parcel contained illegal items
   (commonly: drugs, MDMA, fake documents) and your Aadhaar is linked to it.
3. Threat of arrest; the call is often transferred to a fake "police" or
   "Narcotics" officer (the handoff is a classic tactic).
4. Demand for a "fine," "clearance fee," or transfer to a "verification account,"
   or a demand for personal/bank details. May escalate to digital arrest.

## Red flags
- An unexpected call about a parcel you did not send.
- Claims the parcel contains drugs/illegal items linked to your Aadhaar.
- Being transferred from "courier" to "Customs" to "police" during one call.
- Any demand for a fee, fine, or money transfer to release the parcel or avoid
  arrest.
- Requests for Aadhaar, OTP, or bank details.

## What real couriers and Customs actually do
- Real courier companies do not phone you to say a parcel contains drugs and that
  you will be arrested. Delivery issues are handled via the company's official app/
  website and tracking number.
- Real Customs does not collect fines over the phone or via UPI/bank transfer to a
  personal "verification account."
- No agency asks for your OTP or full bank details to "clear" a parcel.

## Correct advice
- Hang up. Do not press any key, do not "speak to the officer."
- Do not pay any fee or share any details.
- If you genuinely expect a parcel, check only via the courier's official app or
  website using the real tracking number — never a number or link the caller gives.
- Report via 1930 and https://cybercrime.gov.in if money was lost or details shared.

## Reporting
- Helpline **1930** (24x7). Portal **https://cybercrime.gov.in**.
- Report quickly; early reporting improves the chance of freezing transferred funds.

# KYC Update / Account-Block Scam

## What it is
The victim gets an SMS, WhatsApp, or call claiming their bank account, SIM, PAN,
or a wallet (Paytm/PhonePe) will be "blocked" or "suspended" today unless they
"update KYC" immediately. The goal is to make the victim click a phishing link,
share an OTP, or install a remote-access app, leading to account takeover.

## How the scam unfolds
1. Urgent message: "Your account/SIM/PAN will be blocked today. Update KYC now."
2. A link to a fake bank/wallet page, OR a call from a fake "bank official."
3. Victim is asked to enter card/account details, share an OTP, or install an app
   like AnyDesk so the scammer can "help update KYC."
4. With the OTP / remote access, the scammer drains the account.

## Red flags
- Urgent threat that an account/SIM/PAN will be blocked "today" or "within hours."
- A link to update KYC (real banks do not update KYC via random SMS/WhatsApp links).
- Any request for OTP, full card number, CVV, PIN, or net-banking password.
- A request to install AnyDesk / TeamViewer / "support" apps.

## What real banks actually do
- Banks NEVER ask for OTP, PIN, CVV, or full card number by call, SMS, email, or
  link. Their staff will tell you they will never ask for these.
- Real KYC updates are done in the branch or inside the bank's official app, not
  via a link someone sends you.
- Banks do not ask you to install screen-sharing apps.

## Correct advice
- Do not click the link. Do not share OTP/PIN/CVV/card number with anyone.
- Do not install any app on someone's phone instruction.
- If unsure about your KYC, call the number printed on the back of your debit card
  or visit your branch — never the number in the suspicious message.
- If you shared an OTP or card details, call your bank to block the card
  immediately, then report.

## Reporting
- Helpline **1930** (24x7). Portal **https://cybercrime.gov.in**.
- Report within hours for the best chance of freezing/recovering funds.

# UPI / Refund / Wrong-Payment Scams

## What it is
A family of scams abusing UPI (PhonePe, Google Pay, Paytm, BHIM). The common
thread: the scammer tricks the victim into APPROVING a payment or sharing a UPI
PIN, while making it feel like they are RECEIVING money.

## Common variants
- **"Wrong transfer, please return it":** Scammer says they accidentally sent you
  money and asks you to send it back. (Often no money actually arrived, or it was
  a pending request disguised as a credit.)
- **Collect-request trick:** Scammer sends a UPI "request money" / "collect"
  request. If you approve and enter your PIN, money LEAVES your account. You never
  enter a UPI PIN to RECEIVE money.
- **Fake refund/cashback:** "You are eligible for a refund/cashback — scan this QR
  or approve this request." Scanning a QR or approving a request SENDS money.
- **Marketplace/OLX buyer scam:** A "buyer" insists on paying via a QR code or
  collect request, which actually withdraws money from the seller.

## The single most important UPI rule
You NEVER need to enter your UPI PIN, scan a QR code, or approve a "collect
request" to RECEIVE money. Entering your PIN or scanning a QR always SENDS money
FROM your account. If someone says you must do these to "get" money, it is a scam.

## Red flags
- Being asked to enter your UPI PIN, scan a QR, or approve a request to "receive"
  a refund, prize, or returned payment.
- Urgency and a stranger guiding you step-by-step through your UPI app.
- A "buyer" who only pays via QR/collect request.

## Correct advice
- Do not enter your UPI PIN or scan any QR to receive money. Stop and check your
  actual balance independently.
- If someone claims a wrong transfer, ask them to raise it with their own bank/UPI
  app; do not send money on a stranger's word.
- Never approve a collect request from an unknown source.

## Reporting
- Helpline **1930** (24x7). Portal **https://cybercrime.gov.in**.
- Keep the UPI transaction ID / reference number ready when you report.

# Job, Task, Investment & Lottery Scams

## What they are
Scams that lure victims with the promise of money — a job, easy "task" earnings,
high investment returns, or a lottery win — then extract fees or larger
"investments" that are never returned.

## Task / part-time job scam
- Message on WhatsApp/Telegram offering easy money for "liking videos," "rating
  hotels," or simple tasks.
- Small early payouts build trust, then victims are pushed to deposit money for
  "prepaid tasks" / "merchant tasks" with promised higher returns. The deposits
  are lost.
- Red flags: unsolicited job offer, small initial earnings, then a demand to
  deposit your own money to "unlock" bigger earnings.

## Investment / trading scam ("pig butchering")
- A stranger (often via WhatsApp, a dating app, or a stock-tip group) befriends
  the victim and introduces a "guaranteed high return" trading/crypto app or group.
- A fake app shows growing profits; withdrawals are blocked until more "tax,"
  "fee," or "minimum balance" is paid.
- Red flags: guaranteed/very high returns, a slick app you were referred to
  privately, withdrawals blocked behind extra fees, pressure to invest more.

## Lottery / prize / lucky-draw scam
- Call/SMS: "You won Rs X lakh/crore in a KBC/lucky draw — pay a processing fee/
  GST/tax to release the prize."
- Each fee is followed by another. No real prize exists.
- Red flags: you won a lottery you never entered; any demand to pay a fee to
  receive a prize.

## Universal rule for all of these
A legitimate job, investment, or prize NEVER requires you to pay money upfront to
receive money. Any "pay first to earn/withdraw/claim" demand is a scam. Guaranteed
high returns do not exist — that promise itself is the warning sign.

## Correct advice
- Do not pay any upfront fee, deposit, tax, or "processing charge."
- Do not move money into an app or account a stranger referred you to.
- Verify a company/recruiter through official, independent channels; verify
  investment platforms with SEBI-registered status.
- Be wary of guaranteed returns and of online-only "friends" steering you to invest.

## Reporting
- Helpline **1930** (24x7). Portal **https://cybercrime.gov.in**.
- Report early; keep transaction IDs, chat screenshots, and the app/website details.
